content.text: for LLM-powered chains and agents. You drag LangChain/LlamaIndex nodes onto a canvas, wire them together, and Flowise runs the resulting
as a REST API. There is no code to write for the happy path — the entire chain is defined in the UI.

attrs.alt: Flowise — User Input → Chat Model → Agent/Chain (with Vector Store, Custom Tools, built-in tools) → REST API / Chat Widget

content.text:
# Option 1: npx (no install required)
npx flowise start

# Option 2: npm global install
npm install -g flowise
flowise start

# Option 3: Docker
docker run -d --name flowise \
  -p 3000:3000 \
  -v ~/.flowise:/root/.flowise \
  flowiseai/flowise

# Access the canvas at http://localhost:3000
. Flowise persists chatflows in a local SQLite database by default. Switch to PostgreSQL or MySQL via environment variables (
a saved visual graph of nodes. Each chatflow compiles to a LangChain / LlamaIndex pipeline and is served at a unique prediction endpoint.
a reusable component — Chat Model, Memory, Document Loader, Vector Store, Tool, Chain, or Agent. Nodes are visual wrappers around LangChain/LlamaIndex classes.
an encrypted key-value pair (API key, database URL) stored in Flowise and injected into nodes at runtime. Never put secrets in node configuration fields.
that runs one turn of the chatflow for a given question.
that ingests documents into the chatflow's vector store.
Any application can send a question to a chatflow via HTTP. The response contains
object lets you override node parameters at runtime without changing the chatflow in the UI — useful for per-request temperature adjustments or injecting a specific system prompt.
to receive tokens as server-sent events (SSE). Each

import httpx

async def stream_answer(chatflow_id: str, question: str):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        async with client.stream(
            "POST",
            f"http://localhost:3000/api/v1/prediction/{chatflow_id}",
            json={"question": question},
            headers={"Accept": "text/event-stream"},
            timeout=60,
        ) as resp:
            async for line in resp.aiter_lines():
                if line.startswith("data:"):
                    chunk = line[5:].strip()
                    if chunk and chunk != "[DONE]":
                        print(chunk, end="", flush=True)
    print()

import asyncio
asyncio.run(stream_answer("your-uuid", "Explain the refund policy."))
Load documents into the chatflow's vector store without writing ingestion code. The upsert endpoint accepts files and chunked text, then stores embeddings in the configured vector database.

import requests

# Upsert documents into the vector store attached to a chatflow
with open("handbook.pdf", "rb") as f:
    resp = requests.post(
        "http://localhost:3000/api/v1/vector/upsert/your-chatflow-uuid",
        files={"files": ("handbook.pdf", f, "application/pdf")},
        headers={"Authorization": "Bearer your-flowise-api-key"},
    )
print(resp.json())  # { "numAdded": 42, "numUpdated": 0, "numDeleted": 0 }
The chatflow must have a Vector Store node with
enabled. After upsert, the same chatflow's Agent or QA chain can retrieve these chunks automatically during prediction.
The most common Flowise pattern is a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) chatflow. The canvas wiring is:
Chat Model node (choose provider and model)
Document Loader node (PDF / CSV / Web URL)
Vector Store node (Pinecone / Qdrant / PGVector) → wire Document Loader → Text Splitter → Embeddings → Vector Store
Conversational Retrieval QA Chain or Tool Agent node → wire Chat Model + Vector Store Retriever
Buffer Memory node (optional, for multi-turn) → wire to the Chain
The chain node exposes a chat input and produces a chat output. Click
and the prediction endpoint is immediately live.
node lets you write a JavaScript function the Agent can call. Declare the tool's input schema (parameter names and descriptions) in the node's UI; Flowise passes the validated arguments as
. This is the correct way to pass API keys to custom tools — never hardcode them.

Flowise generates a JavaScript snippet that embeds a chat bubble on any web page. The widget connects directly to the prediction API, handling streaming, session management, and branding via configuration.

<!-- Embed the Flowise chat widget in any web page -->
<script type="module">
  import Chatbot from 'https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/flowise-embed/dist/web.js';
  Chatbot.init({
    chatflowid: 'your-chatflow-uuid',
    apiHost: 'http://localhost:3000',
    chatflowConfig: {
      temperature: 0.5,
    },
    theme: {
      button: { backgroundColor: '#3b82f6' },
      chatWindow: {
        welcomeMessage: 'Hi! Ask me anything about our products.',
        fontSize: 16,
      },
    },
  });
</script>
object maps to the chatflow's overrideConfig. Use it to inject the current user's language preference, product context, or other per-session variables.
Protect your prediction endpoint with Flowise API keys. Generate keys in
and restrict individual chatflows to specific keys in

# Set API key auth in Flowise (via the UI: Settings → API Keys → + Add Key)
# Then pass the key in requests:

import requests

resp = requests.post(
    "http://localhost:3000/api/v1/prediction/your-chatflow-uuid",
    json={"question": "Hello"},
    headers={"Authorization": "Bearer flowise-xxxxxxxx"},
)
print(resp.json()["text"])

# Restrict a chatflow to specific API keys in:
# Chatflow Settings → Security → Allowed API Keys
For public-facing widgets embedded in a webpage, enable
and whitelist the allowed origin domain in the chatflow settings — this prevents other sites from calling your endpoint with the chatflow ID.
to maintain conversation history across API calls. Flowise stores the Buffer Memory keyed to this ID.
) rather than a random UUID per call. A random UUID starts a fresh conversation every time — the memory node is not consulted.
Comparison: Flowise vs n8n vs Code
the chatflow ID in the embed snippet is not a secret — restrict the endpoint with API key auth and origin whitelisting instead of treating the ID as a password.
the vector store caches the retriever in memory. After upserting new documents, clear the chatflow cache (
button in the chatflow editor) or restart Flowise for changes to take effect.
Flowise bundles specific versions of LangChain. Nodes available in the UI may not match your locally installed LangChain version if you customise the Docker image. Pin the Flowise version to match.
default Buffer Memory is in-process. It resets on Flowise restart. Use Redis-backed memory or an external database memory node for production persistence.
uploading large PDF batches via the upsert API can exceed the default HTTP timeout. Split files into smaller batches or increase

nodes where a supervisor agent orchestrates specialist sub-agents. Each sub-agent is a separate Agent node with its own system prompt and tool set; the supervisor decides which sub-agent to call based on the task.

Connect the Supervisor Agent's output to each sub-agent's input, then wire sub-agent outputs back to the Supervisor. The Supervisor uses its LLM to decide the call order — it is not a hard-coded switch statement.
Multi-agent chatflows are more expensive than single-agent ones because every sub-agent call is a separate LLM inference. Profile token usage in the Flowise execution logs before deploying at scale.

Flowise chatflows can be exported as JSON and committed to git. The REST API allows programmatic import, making it possible to build a CI/CD pipeline that deploys chatflows alongside your application code.

# Export a chatflow to JSON (for version control)
# In Flowise UI: Chatflow menu → Export Chatflow
# Saves as: chatflow-name.json

# Import a chatflow from JSON
# In Flowise UI: + New → Import Chatflow → select .json file

# For CI/CD: use the Flowise REST API
import requests

with open("my-chatflow.json") as f:
    chatflow_json = f.read()

# Create/update via API
resp = requests.post(
    "http://localhost:3000/api/v1/chatflows",
    data=chatflow_json,
    headers={
        "Authorization": "Bearer your-api-key",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
    },
)
print(resp.json()["id"])
Store chatflow JSON files in a
directory in your repository. Use the import API in your deployment script to synchronise chatflows between environments (dev → staging → production).
For production, switch from the default SQLite database to PostgreSQL and set a strong
to encrypt credentials. Add username/password authentication to protect the UI.

# docker-compose.yml for production Flowise deployment
version: '3.8'
services:
  flowise:
    image: flowiseai/flowise:latest
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"
    volumes:
      - flowise_data:/root/.flowise
    environment:
      # Auth
      - FLOWISE_USERNAME=admin
      - FLOWISE_PASSWORD=strongpassword
      # Database (PostgreSQL)
      - DATABASE_TYPE=postgres
      - DATABASE_HOST=postgres
      - DATABASE_PORT=5432
      - DATABASE_NAME=flowise
      - DATABASE_USER=flowise
      - DATABASE_PASSWORD=dbpass
      # Encryption key (keep secret)
      - FLOWISE_SECRETKEY_OVERWRITE=your-32-char-secret-key
      # File upload limit
      - FLOWISE_FILE_SIZE_LIMIT=50mb
    depends_on:
      - postgres

  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      - POSTGRES_DB=flowise
      - POSTGRES_USER=flowise
      - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=dbpass
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data

volumes:
  flowise_data:
  postgres_data:
key encrypts credentials stored in the database. If you lose it, all stored API keys and tokens become unreadable — back it up in a secrets manager before deploying.
Flowise does not support stateful horizontal scaling out of the box. Run a single instance behind a load balancer for high availability, or use Flowise Cloud for managed scaling.

inside the container. Mount this path as a persistent volume or use external object storage (S3) via the
Flowise is a visual LangChain/LlamaIndex builder: drag nodes onto a canvas, wire them, and get an instant REST prediction API.
RAG chatflows are built by chaining Document Loader → Text Splitter → Embeddings → Vector Store → QA Chain, all in the UI.
Custom Tool nodes run JavaScript functions and expose them as agent tools with declared parameter schemas.
The embed snippet deploys a branded chat widget to any webpage with three lines of HTML.
Use deterministic, user-scoped session IDs to maintain multi-turn memory across API calls.
